title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a glaciologist called Alfie Brooks talking about his research into melting ice sheets. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a glaciologist called Alfie Brooks talking about his research into melting ice sheets. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Alfie Brooks explains the technical methods used to measure glacial retreat and the implications for the planet.
extracts.audio_script:
Hello, I’m Alfie. Most people see a glacier as a static block of ice, but to a glaciologist, they are living, moving entities. My interest in the cryosphere didn't start with a textbook; it actually began when I saw a [7]time-lapse video[/7] of a retreating glacier in Greenland during a geography lesson at school. Seeing miles of ice disappear in just a few minutes of footage was a wake-up call. I wanted to understand the mechanics behind that collapse.

Measuring glaciers is a massive logistical challenge. We use satellite data, but to get the real story, you have to be on the ground. I’ve found that [8]drilling ice cores[/8] is the most physically demanding part of our expeditions. We extract long cylinders of ice that contain air bubbles trapped thousands of years ago. These bubbles are essentially 'time capsules' that tell us exactly what the atmosphere was like in the distant past. It’s the most accurate record of ancient climate we have.

One of the most surprising things we’ve discovered is how water moves *under* the ice. We used to think glaciers slid slowly, but we’ve noticed that [9]meltwater lakes[/9] on the surface can suddenly drain through cracks, acting like a lubricant. This causes the glacier to surge towards the ocean much faster than we previously predicted. It’s a bit like a giant water slide, and it’s significantly accelerating sea-level rise.

Technology is helping us monitor these changes in real-time. We now deploy 'cryo-sensors' that can withstand extreme pressure. I’ve noticed that the [10]internal temperature[/10] of the ice sheet is rising faster than the air temperature above it. This 'internal warming' is making the ice much more brittle. It’s a worrying trend that suggests the ice sheets are becoming increasingly unstable.

Working in the Arctic requires extreme mental focus. I’ve found that the [11]constant daylight[/11] during the summer months is the hardest thing for new researchers to adapt to. It completely disrupts your sleep cycle and can lead to significant exhaustion. You have to be very disciplined about your schedule, or you’ll find yourself working at 3:00 AM without realizing it.

Despite the harsh conditions, there is a strange beauty in the landscape. I’m also a keen sound recordist. I’ve found that the [12]popping sounds[/12] made by melting ice are incredibly distinct. It’s actually the sound of ancient air being released under pressure. I’m currently building an archive of these sounds to help people connect emotionally with the changing environment through art installations.

Looking forward, I’m concerned about the impact on coastal cities. I believe that [13]improved satellite mapping[/13] is our best defense, as it allows us to predict flood risks with much greater accuracy. We need to give governments the data they need to build better defenses before it’s too late.

Finally, I’m currently finishing a [14]documentary script[/14] that follows our latest expedition to the Antarctic. I hope it will make the science accessible to a wider audience. We need everyone to understand what’s happening at the poles. Thanks for listening.
